sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: LLM-based agents increasingly operate in persistent environments where they must store, update, and reason over information across many sessions. While prior benchmarks evaluate only single-entity updates, MEME defines six tasks spanning the full space defined by the multi-entity and evolving axes, including three not scored by prior work: Cascade and Absence (dependency reasoning) and Deletion (post-removal state). Evaluating six memory systems spanning three memory paradigms on 100 controlled episodes, we find that all systems collapse on dependency reasoning under the default configuration (Cascade: 3%, Absence: 1% in average accuracy) despite adequate static retrieval performance. Prompt optimization, deeper retrieval, reduced filler noise, and most stronger LLMs fail to close this gap. Only a file-based agent paired with Claude Opus 4.7 as its internal LLM partially closes the gap, but at ~70x the baseline cost, indicating closure currently depends on configurations that are not practical at scale. Code and data are available on the project page: https://seokwonjung-jay.github.io/meme-eval/.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: As Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly serve as agents that interact with users across many sessions, accurately storing, updating, and reasoning over past interactions has become essential [17]. For instance, when user reports moving to new city, the agent must not only record this change but also recognize that previously stored facts that depended on the old residence, such as commute time or nearby facilities, may no longer be valid. Todays memory systems address such needs through three broad paradigms: raw retrieval over unprocessed text chunks [6], LLM-processed memory that extracts and reorganizes facts [1, 3, 12, 13], and file-based agents where an LLM manages persistent files via tool-calling [7]. Evaluation for these systems has evolved from single-turn long-context benchmarks [4, 11] through multi-session evaluations of static fact retention [10] to benchmarks with dynamic updates, where entity values change across sessions [5, 15, 16]. Figure 1: MEMEs taxonomy of memory operations along two dimensions: entity scope (single vs. multi-entity) and temporal dynamics (static vs. evolving), with six tasks distributed across the four quadrants. Yet existing memory benchmarks evaluate updates only for independent entities, missing the dependency reasoning that real interactions require  (Table 1)  . No prior benchmark scores how dependent *Equal contribution. Preprint. Figure 2: Examples of the six MEME task types across three categories: Left: Retrieval (Exact Recall, Aggregation), which merges the Single-Fact Retrieval and Multi-Fact Retrieval quadrants of Figure 1. Middle: State Management (Tracking, Deletion). Right: Dependency Reasoning (Cascade, Absence). Each distinct entity is rendered in different color. fact changes after an upstream update (Cascade), how previously valid answer becomes uncertain (Absence), or how removed fact stops being reported (Deletion). This leaves critical blind spot in how todays memory systems handle stateful, interdependent knowledge. Table 1: Comparison between MEME and existing memory benchmarks. Tasks: Exact Recall (ER), Aggregation (Agg), Tracking (Tr), Deletion (Del), Cascade (Cas), Absence (Abs). Mappings are approximated from reported task types. coverage; absence. To address this gap, we argue that complete memory evaluation must be organized along two orthogonal dimensions: entity scope (single vs. multi-entity) and temporal dynamics (static vs. evolving). These dimensions reflect known challenges in related fields: the entity scope axis parallels the single-hop vs. multi-hop distinction in question answering [18, 21], while the temporal dynamics axis parallels the ripple effect problem in knowledge editing, where modifying one fact requires propagating changes to logically dependent facts [19, 20]. While these axes have been studied separately, real interactions combine them: an update to one entity can ripple through multiple dependents over time, making joint evaluation along both axes essential. RULER [4] NoLiMa [11] LoCoMo [10] LongMemEval [16] MemBench [15] MemoryAgentBench [5] ER Agg Tr Del Cas Abs MEME (Ours) Based on this framework, we present MEME (Multi-entity and Evolving Memory Evaluation), benchmark that defines six tasks targeting memory-intensive operations in each quadrant of this two-dimensional space (Figure 1). Our contributions are: principled evaluation taxonomy. We organize memory evaluation along entity scope temporal dynamics and select representative tasks per quadrant, including Cascade (inferring unstated changes from dependency rules), Absence (recognizing that previously valid answer is no longer trustworthy), and Deletion (verifying that removed fact is no longer reported), task types that no existing benchmark scores. 2 rigorously controlled dataset with verifiable and solvable ground truth. We generate episodes from DAG-based knowledge graph with explicit conditional rules across two domains (Personal Life and Software Project); the DAG structure makes gold answers verifiable by construction, and an in-context validation (gold facts fed directly to the answering LLM) confirms the tasks are solvable in principle. diagnostic study of where current memory systems fail and where closure does emerge. We evaluate six systems spanning three architectural paradigms and find: (i) every practical-cost configuration fails Cas/Abs (Cascade: 0.03, Absence: 0.01 in average accuracy), and the gap persists under prompt optimization, deeper retrieval, stronger answering LLM, and reduced filler noise; (ii) most systems encode and retain the dependency rule and the change event in their stores, but at retrieval the change event is either out-ranked by the value held before the change on vector retrievers, or never surfaced at all on tool-use, graph, and sparse retrievers, so the answering LLM reports that earlier value; (iii) closure does emerge when MD-flat uses Opus 4.7 as its internal LLM, where this internal LLM writes the propagated value into the store at ingest so the retriever surfaces it directly, but at 70 the baseline cost.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: We review prior work on memory system architectures and on the benchmarks used to evaluate them, framing the gap that MEME addresses. Memory architectures. LLM memory systems span three paradigms. Raw retrieval stores session text as chunks and retrieves via lexical (BM25) or semantic similarity [6], preserving original utterances but bounded by fixed top-k window. LLM-processed memory uses an internal LLM during ingestion to extract or restructure: Mem0 [1] decomposes conversations into atomic facts [22], MemGPT [12] pages between working memory and external storage, Graphiti [13], the open-source temporal-knowledge-graph engine underlying Zep, encodes entity-relation triples, and GraphRAG [3] adds community summaries. File-based agents hand LLMs tool-calling access to persistent markdown stores curated across sessions, including Hermes1, OpenClaw2, and the Karpathy Wiki [7]. These three paradigms span the systems we evaluate; we benchmark all of them on dependency reasoning and find that no practical-cost configuration closes the gap. Memory benchmarks. Stateless probes like RULER [4] and NoLiMa [11] measure attentionwindow limits within single input rather than persistent memory across sessions. Multi-session benchmarks evaluate memory across sessions: LoCoMo [10] tests retention of static preferences, while LongMemEval [16], MemBench [15], and MemoryAgentBench [5] extend evaluation to evolving memory through knowledge updates, abstention, aggregation, and selective forgetting. These tasks remain isolated, single-entity updates and do not evaluate the ripple effects an upstream change should trigger in dependent entities. MEME differs by scoring three task types absent in prior work (Cascade, Absence, Deletion), which are constructed from DAG-based knowledge graph with verifiable propagation gold answers.

sections.title: 3 MEME
sections.content: MEME maps the entity-scope temporal-dynamics framework to six tasks (Section 3.1) and DAG-based generation pipeline that yields verifiable gold answers across 100 episodes (Section 3.2).

sections.title: 3.1 Task Definitions
sections.content: Within each quadrant of the entity scope temporal dynamics space (Figure 1), we select one or two memory-intensive operations from those commonly encountered in long-running agent deployments as the representative task(s); easier variants are already covered by existing benchmarks and are intentionally excluded. Concrete examples of all six tasks are illustrated in Figure 2. Exact Recall (ER) targets single static entity and demands character-level verbatim reproduction, testing encoding fidelity. Aggregation (Agg) combines multiple static entities scattered across 1https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent 2https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw 3 separate sessions into single answer, testing retrieval coverage when no explicit link connects them. Tracking (Tr) reconstructs the full revision history of single evolving entity in chronological order, testing whether past values are retained rather than overwritten. Deletion (Del) tests whether the system stops reporting fact after the user explicitly removes it, rather than continuing to surface the old value. Cascade (Cas) infers that dependent entitys value has changed based on stated dependency rule and an upstream update, testing propagation through dependency chains. Absence (Abs) recognizes that dependent entity is uncertain after an upstream change with no replacement rule, where the correct answer is uncertainty rather than new value.

sections.title: 3.2 Dataset Generation
sections.content: We generate the MEME dataset across two domains, Personal Life (PL; everyday interactions with personal assistant) and Software Project (SW; collaborative planning of software project), in two stages. First, we define knowledge graph per domain that encodes entities and their dependencies. Then, we construct evaluation episodes by sampling entity subsets, assigning tasks, verbalizing facts into conversations, and assembling filler-interleaved haystacks. Knowledge graph. Each domain is built on Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) = (V, E, P, Φ), where is set of entities (e.g., health_condition, medication), contains directed dependency edges (e.g., health_condition medication), P(v) is the value pool for entity v, and Φ is set of conditional rules. Each rule ϕv Φ specifies how descendant vs value depends on its parents (e.g., if health condition changes to high blood pressure, switch medication to Thrynexol). The dataset comprises 100 evaluation episodes (50 per domain). Each domain uses single handcrafted knowledge graph reused across episodes (Personal Life: 39 entities, 34 edges; Software Project: 51, 27; full breakdown in Table 7). Each episode is 35K tokens of dialogue context and yields 694 post-change evaluation questions across the six task types (332 PL + 362 SW). All entity values use fictitious names to prevent parametric knowledge contamination; graph details in Section B. Episode construction. Each episode is tuple ϵ = (G, S, Q, A), where is chronological sequence of conversational sessions, is the set of evaluation questions, and is the corresponding gold answers. For Cascade and Absence tasks, the gold answer is not stated in but is computed by propagating updates through G. When parent vi is updated in S, the resolved state of dependent is: = (cid:26)ϕv(v ) if ϕv Φ (Cascade) if ϕv / Φ (Absence) (1) Here denotes that no answer is derivable from the available rules; the gold answer for Absence is Uncertain. The definition is recursive: for chain v1 v2, 2 = ϕv2 (v 1), so single root change propagates through multi-hop chains. The gold answer for these tasks is = v. We refer to the value of stated in before the upstream change as the pre-change value, in contrast to the resolved v. 1 = ϕv1 (r) and We construct each episode in five steps over the fixed graph G: 1. Entity set selection. root entity is selected from G. The episode uses this root, its descendants in G, and sample of entities from outside its cascade chain. 2. Value assignment. Each entity in the episode is assigned an initial value from its value pool P(v). Domain-specific consistency constraints are applied as post-processing pass to ensure the initial graph state is logically coherent. For example, if vehicle is none, commute_method excludes driving. 3. Task assignment. Entities are mapped to task types based on their topological role in G: Tracking: entities outside the cascade chain, with three value updates across the episode. Cascade and Absence: sampled from the roots descendants. Aggregation: predefined triples drawn from descendants and entities outside the cascade chain. Exact Recall and Deletion: entities outside the cascade chain. 4 4. Verbalization. We employ hybrid approach to convert the structured skeleton into conversational sessions. Base facts are converted into multi-turn dialogues via LLM self-chat (gpt-4o), where User LLM and an Assistant LLM alternate turns to produce natural conversation from structured fact seeds (full session in Section B.3). In contrast, dependency rules and exact recall facts are embedded using template-direct (verbatim) text to ensure absolute factual precision. two-layer LLM verification pass (gpt-4o annotation, Gemini 2.5 Flash semantic audit) confirms that all self-chat turns faithfully reflect the underlying gold facts (details in Section D.2). 5. Haystack assembly. Evidence sessions Sev are interleaved with filler sessions Sfill. To prevent semantic interference, we use an offline pre-processing pipeline where domain-matched corpus is filtered using hybrid retrieval-and-conflict-removal strategy (BM25 and text-embedding3-small surface candidates for gpt-4o-mini conflict judge). During final assembly, we apply keyword-based blocklist to select pre-filtered fillers that do not clash with the gold facts of the current episode. The resulting episodes contain approximately 35,000 tokens. Full filtering pipeline, statistics, and rejection examples are in Section B.4.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: We evaluate six memory systems on MEME and find that all of them fail Cascade and Absence. We then ask two questions in turn: where in each system the dependency information is lost (Section 4.3), and whether we can close the gap without changing the memory architecture (Section 4.4). One configuration does close the gap, and we end that section with case study of what made it possible and what it costs.

sections.title: 4.1 Experimental Setup
sections.content: Systems and LLM roles. We evaluate six memory systems spanning the three paradigms identified in Section 2: raw retrieval (BM25 [9], text-embedding-3-small), LLM-processed memory (Mem0 [1], Graphiti [13]), and file-based agents (Karpathy Wiki [7] and MD-flat). Per-system configurations are in Section C; ingestion, retrieval, and answer prompts are in Section D. The Karpathy Wiki uses an LLM to extract knowledge from each session into dated daily logs and periodically compiles those logs into topic-specific concept articles that retrieval reads from, while MD-flat (our minimal single-file baseline) keeps all facts in single markdown file curated through read/write/append tool calls. All systems ingest identical chronological session transcripts and use gpt-4.1-mini uniformly in two roles: as the internal LLM (used inside the memory system for ingestion, extraction, or retrieval planning) and as the answering LLM (which produces the final user-facing answer from retrieved context). This places every system on the same language-model footing and isolates differences in memory architecture. Five systems issue the two roles as separate LLM calls, while Karpathy Wiki performs both within single agentic loop. We additionally include an in-context baseline that bypasses the memory system and feeds the entire 32K-filler episode transcript directly to the answering LLM (gpt-4.1-mini and Sonnet 4.6). This baseline anchors the cost-efficiency reference for memory architectures, which trade one-time ingestion overhead for cheaper per-query inference. Memory pipeline. We refer to three stages within each memory system that we will reuse throughout the analysis: encoding (writing each user-stated fact and conditional rule into the store at ingestion), maintenance (retaining the rule and any subsequent change events in the store up to query time), and retrieval (surfacing that content for the answering LLM at query time). Table 11 maps each system across these stages plus its storage substrate. Evaluation protocol. Answer correctness is evaluated by GPT-4o judge [23], validated against the authors annotations on 144 samples (98.6% agreement, Cohens κ [2] of 0.965); task-specific judge prompts are in Section D.5. For Cascade, Absence, and Deletion tasks, we apply trivial-pass filtering: credit requires correct answers both before and after the change or delete event. For example, on Deletion task where the user first says their hobby is pottery and later asks to remove that fact, the system is credited only if it recalls pottery beforehand and stops reporting it afterward. This excludes false positives from systems that never encoded the fact. gold-facts in-context ceiling, where only task-relevant gold facts are fed directly to the answering LLM, confirms that the tasks are solvable in principle: 0.91 overall with Claude Opus 4.7 (full breakdown across four answer LLMs in Section L). sections.title: 4.2 Main Results
sections.content: Table 2 summarizes accuracy across all six tasks. We highlight three findings below. No system reliably solves dependency reasoning. The best system (MD-flat) reaches only 0.42 overall. Dependency reasoning is the most consistent failure: Cascade averages 0.03 and Absence 0.01 across all six systems, well below the per-task averages on every static task (the lowest is Aggregation at 0.23). This failure is consistent across all three paradigms and stable across =5 samplings (Section G). (a) temporal axis (b) entity-scope axis (c) both crossed The two evaluation axes shape system performance. Both axes substantially reduce mean accuracy on their own (entity scope 0.31, temporal 0.28), so neither is redundant; crossing both pushes the Multi-Evolving cell to the floor (0.02, Figure 3). Figure 3: Marginal effect of each evaluation axis on mean accuracy across the six main-table systems. Each axis individually drops accuracy by 0.30; crossing both leaves the Multi-Evolving cell at 0.02 floor. In-context wins on accuracy, memory wins on cost-efficiency at scale. In-context queries on gpt-4.1-mini reach Overall 0.36, outperforming five of the six memory systems (only MD-flat at 0.42 does better). However, in-contexts per-query inference cost ($0.16/ep) exceeds most memory systems ($0.00$0.04/ep for raw retrieval, Mem0, MD-flat), so memory systems become more cost-efficient as query volume grows.

sections.title: 4.3 How dependency reasoning fails
sections.content: Figure 4 traces representative Cascade episode through Graphiti and Karpathy Wiki, illustrating two distinct retrieval failure mechanisms. Graphiti encodes the conditional rule, the pre-change value, and the change event as edges; at query time, however, its graph search surfaces only the rule and the pre-change value, while the change-event edge falls below the retrieved top-k. Karpathy retains the change event in its daily log, but the query agent navigates only to the rule + pre-change source and never opens the daily log containing the change event. The remaining four systems split into two failure modes (per-system traces in Section I). BM25 and MD-flat (gpt-4.1-mini) are retrieval failures: the change event is below the top-k for BM25 and never opened by the tool-use loop for 6 pre-change value conditional rule change event answer Episode sw_033 (Software Project, Cascade). sections.title: 4.4 Closing the gap without changing the architecture
sections.content: Section 4.3 localizes the gap to the retrieval stage. We now test whether five interventions can close it without changing the memory architecture: prompt optimization, increased retrieval depth, stronger answering LLM, reduced filler noise, and stronger internal LLM. Except for the answering-LLM swap, all ablations use Sonnet 4 as the answering LLM to isolate memory-system effects from the answer LLMs reasoning ceiling. 7 Table 3: Two intervention sweeps: (a) top-k retrieval depth on raw retrieval and Mem0 (single-seed, 40-episode subset); (b) answering-LLM swap (gpt-4.1-mini Sonnet 4) on all six main-table systems (100 episodes). Full per-task answer-LLM numbers in Table 16. We applied DSPy SIMBA [8], an append-only prompt optimizer, to MD-flat, Mem0, Graphiti, and Karpathy Wiki, optimizing each systems ingest and retrieve prompts (single-seed run on 10-episode SIMBA test set; details in Section E). Across all four systems, Cascade and Absence remain at or near the floor (Figure 5; SIMBA configuration in Table 12, MD-flat multi-seed statistics in Table 15). For three systems (MD-flat, Graphiti, Karpathy Wiki), the winning candidate appended advice explicitly targeting dependency failure modes (verbatim in Section E.2); for Mem0, the winning candidate was the librarys default extract prompt unchanged. Cas/Abs stays at the floor across all four, indicating the gap is structural rather than instructional. Increased retrieval depth does not help on Cascade. For BM25, text-embedding-3-small, and Mem0, we sweep top-k across {5, 10, 20, 40} on 40-episode subset to test whether dependency evidence is simply buried below the cutoff. Cascade remains near zero at every value across all three systems  (Table 3)  . Absence on the raw-retrieval systems rises with k, peaking at k=20 (BM25 0.24, dense 0.23) before declining. Per-failure inspection (Section J) shows that at k=20 and k=40 both the rule and the change session are already in the retrieval context for 45% of Cascade failures and 84% of Absence failures. Deeper retrieval thus saturates against an answering-side bottleneck on Absence and roughly even split on Cascade. Mem0 stays at the floor for both Cas and Abs at every k. stronger answering LLM does not consistently help. We replace the answering LLM (gpt-4.1mini Claude Sonnet 4) on all six main-table systems and 100 episodes (Table 3; full per-task breakdown in Table 16). BM25 and text-embedding-3-small show small Absence gains (0.00 0.12 and 0.00 0.16), but Cascade does not improve on any system (averages 0.03 0.02). capable answering LLM cannot reconstruct dependencies that the memory layer never preserved. Reducing noise does not close the gap. We compare the default 32K-filler condition against no-filler condition on the highest-overall system within each paradigm: MD-flat for file-based, Mem0 for LLM-processed, and text-embedding-3-small for raw retrieval (per Table 2). Cascade and Absence remain at or near the floor in both conditions (Figure 5b). The full three-condition sweep (no filler, 32K, 128K) is in Figure 29. Internal-LLM swap is the only intervention that narrows the gap, in one cell. Swapping the internal LLM (gpt-4.1-mini gpt-5, GLM-5.1, or Claude Opus 4.7) on Graphiti, Mem0, and MD-flat over 20-episode subset leaves Cascade and Absence near the floor across most cells. The exception is MD-flat with Opus 4.7, which reaches Cascade 0.32 and Absence 0.59  (Table 4)  . The mechanism is illustrated in the MD-flat Opus 4.7 panel of Figure 4 and detailed in Section K.2. At ingest, Opus writes the current value and an explicit contingency entry naming the parent. When the change later arrives, it scans for dependent contingency entries and writes the propagated value in place. The propagated fact is stand-alone declarative that the retriever ranks at the top of the next query and the answering LLM reads directly, bypassing both the retrieval and answering failures of Section 4.3. sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: Long-running agentic systems demand more from memory than retrieval over fact store. As userstated context evolves, an agents memory must propagate updates through related facts and signal uncertainty when no resolution rule is available. MEME tests this through Cascade and Absence, two dependency-reasoning task types that no prior benchmark covers. Among the practical-cost systems we evaluated, none solves dependency reasoning. Across all six systems the dependency rule and the change event are written and retained, but at retrieval the change event is either out-ranked by the pre-change value (vector retrievers) or never surfaced at all (tool-use, graph, sparse retrievers), so the answering LLM ends up reporting the pre-change value. Closure does emerge, but only with frontier LLM (Opus 4.7) on file-based substrate, where the internal LLM writes the propagated value into the store at ingest so the retriever can surface it directly; this configuration costs 70 the baseline and is not deployable today. Our per-stage diagnosis suggests two deployment patterns. For retrieval-heavy or static workloads, existing systems (BM25, Mem0, MD-flat) suffice. For dependency-heavy workloads, no practical-cost option exists today; careful upstream design (writing dependency rules into the conversation log, surfacing change events through pre-defined templates) is the near-term workaround. Looking further out, the path forward is memory architectures that natively propagate updates through dependent facts at maintenance, rather than relying on costly internal LLM to do so. We leave the architecture open; MEME provides the diagnostic for tracking progress.

sections.title: 6 Limitations
sections.content: Several limitations bound our findings. The dataset uses two hand-crafted knowledge graphs (Personal Life, Software Project) authored by us; broader domains and crowd-sourced graphs would test generalization further. Dialogues are LLM-generated rather than collected from real users, which constrains realism. We evaluate on 100 episodes with 35K-token contexts; longer contexts and larger sample sizes may surface different patterns. Most ablation studies (prompt optimization, top-k sweep, noise ablation, internal-LLM swap) run on 1040 episode subsets and cover only subset of the six memory systems due to compute cost; only the main results and answering-LLM swap cover all 100 episodes across all six systems. Verbalization uses explicit conditional phrasing for dependency rules as best-case framing for memory systems; we have not ablated implicit-conditional or no-conditional variants. All data is English.

sections.title: B Dataset Construction Details
sections.content: Table 7: MEME dataset statistics overview, per domain. (a) Knowledge graph structure: each domain is built on hand-crafted DAG with the entity-type breakdown shown. (b) Episode generation and evaluation statistics. (a) Knowledge graph (b) Episodes and evaluation Personal Life Software Project Total entities Root Middle Leaf Orphan Dependency edges 39 5 5 19 10 34 51 5 6 21 19 27 Personal Life Software Project Number of episodes Evidence sessions / epi. Filler sessions / epi. Filler tokens / epi. Total questions 50 5 18 32K 50 5 14.9 32K 362 12 B.1 Knowledge Graph Structure Each domains knowledge graph = (V, E, P) is hand-crafted by the authors once and reused across all episodes. The structure summary appears in Table 7(a). Personal Life domain. The personal life graph contains 39 entities organized into 9 categories  (Table 8)  . The 5 rotation root entities are: residence_location, employer, health_condition, relationship_status, and school. Dependencies follow 11 semantic patterns: proximity (5 edges), company_policy (5), life_event (6), medical_causation (5), priority_shift (4), infrastructure (3), distance (2), activity_facility (1), schedule_conflict (1), preference (1), and curriculum (1). Each pattern provides natural-language template that binds target fact to its source entity with an explicit conditional dependency clause (e.g., the proximity pattern generates sentences of the form {target_fact} this depends on where {source_entity_phrase}; if move, this would change). Table 8: Personal Life domain: entity categories. Category Entities Living & Commute Work Health & Fitness Food & Diet Family Finance Schedule Hobbies Miscellaneous residence_location, housing_type, living_arrangement, commute_method, commute_duration, vehicle employer, job_title, work_location, work_schedule, work_project health_condition, medication, exercise_routine, fitness_facility, sleep_pattern diet_preference, dietary_restriction, favorite_restaurant, food_allergy relationship_status, partner, family_event financial_goal, insurance, subscription_service, planned_purchase travel_plan, regular_appointment, upcoming_event hobby, sports, club_membership, media_consumption, social_gathering pet, school, skill_acquisition, life_philosophy Software Project domain. The software project graph contains 51 entities organized into 6 categories  (Table 9)  . The 5 root entities are: framework, database, deploy_target, team_lead, and auth_provider. Dependencies follow 6 semantic patterns: tech_compatibility (4 edges), derived_config (6), data_layer (4), infra_coupling (5), team_assignment (4), and auth_coupling (4). All entity values use fictitious names manually authored by the authors (e.g., Veltrion for framework, Crysthene DB for database, Narvex Cloud for deploy target, Verithos Auth for auth provider), chosen to sound plausible within their respective domains while avoiding collision with real products. This prevents parametric knowledge contamination, as the LLM cannot have encountered these names during pre-training. The same value pool is reused across all 100 episodes for consistency. Table 9: Software Project domain: entity categories. Category Entities Framework & Build Data Layer Deployment & Infra Team & Process Auth & Security Miscellaneous framework, build_tool, build_command, test_framework, test_command, project_structure, dev_server_command database, orm_library, model_syntax, migration_tool, connection_string, backup_schedule deploy_target, ci_config, deploy_command, monitoring_url, staging_url, docker_image, dockerfile_path, log_drain_endpoint team_lead, code_reviewer, escalation_contact, approval_authority, weekly_report_recipient auth_provider, auth_method, middleware_config, token_format, login_endpoint, user_session_ttl error_log, sprint_deadline, secret_manager, package_manager, branch_strategy, design_system, meeting_day, slack_channel, standup_format, default_branch, release_cadence, test_coverage_target, code_review_policy, oncall_rotation, alert_channel, incident_runbook_url, documentation_url, onboarding_guide, changelog_location B.2 Entity Values and Episode Generation Each entity has finite value pool P(v) from which values are sampled. Pool sizes range from 5 to 15 values per entity. All pools are manually curated to ensure values are mutually distinguishable and semantically appropriate for their entity type. Root entities have pools of 612 values to support diverse episode generation across 100 episodes without excessive repetition. The complete value pools are provided in the released dataset files. 13 Null-like values (e.g., none, none currently, no pet, not currently enrolled) are included in some pools but are excluded from task assignment via NULL_VALUES filter. These values cannot appear as beforeor after-values in cascade, update, or tracking tasks. Episode generation follows the five-step process described in Section 3.2. root entity is selected from the graph, cycling through all 5 roots before reuse. The cascade chain is constructed by traversing the dependency graph outward from the root. At each hop level, two types of targets are selected: one Cascade target (with declared if-then replacement rule) and one Absence target (without replacement value). Both 1-hop and 2-hop descendants are considered, yielding up to 4 dependency targets per episode (2 Cascade + 2 Absence). If 2-hop chain does not exist for the selected root, the episode contains only 1-hop dependency targets. Entities not participating in the cascade chain are assigned to stateless tasks. Exact recall selects entities explicitly marked as exact_recall in the entity pool. These are entities with long, complex values that require verbatim reproduction (life_philosophy for personal life, error_log for software project). Deletion selects an orphan entity whose value is personally meaningful. Aggregation selects from set of manually curated triples of semantically related but independently stored entities (e.g., hobby + sports + club_membership for What do do in my free time?). Tracking selects from predefined set of entities suitable for multi-value history (e.g., vehicle, media_consumption, partner for personal life; sprint_deadline, secret_manager, package_manager for software project) and assigns 3 chronologically ordered values. Values are sampled uniformly from each entitys pool, subject to domain-specific consistency constraints applied as post-processing pass. For dependency entities, the after-value for Cascade targets is determined by the declared if-then rule, while Absence targets have their after-value set to (unknown). Each generated episode must contain all 6 required task types. If any task type is missing, the entire episode is discarded and regenerated with different root (up to 20 retries). B.3 Example Generated Session To illustrate the self-chat verbalization described in Section 3.2, Figure 6 shows full generated session from Episode 1 of the Personal Life domain. This session is the Fact Introduction (Part 1a) of the episode, with root entity health_condition. It encodes five gold facts: hobby = pottery, vehicle = Zyvanta Sedan, health_condition = lactose intolerance, sleep_pattern = varies by shift, and media_consumption = The Quorath Codex (book). Assistant turns are abbreviated to their first sentence for space; the User LLM interleaves each fact as natural conversational opener. B.4 Haystack Assembly and Filler Filtering Filler sessions are drawn from two public corpora: LongMemEval-S [16] haystack sessions for Personal Life (17,034 sessions after extracting non-evidence sessions from each instance and deduplicating by session ID), and the ShareGPT coding English subset [14] for Software Project (29,502 conversations). length filter (5005,000 estimated tokens per session) removes overly short or long fillers, yielding raw pools of 1,130 (Personal Life) and 9,135 (Software Project) sessions. Filtering pipeline. We then filter each raw pool against that domains gold-fact vocabulary through four-step pipeline: 1. Enumerate all possible gold-fact sentences by applying each entitys value pool to its fact template. 2. For each gold fact, retrieve the top-K=10 candidate fillers using hybrid of BM25 lexical scoring and text-embedding-3-small dense similarity. 3. Judge each (gold fact, filler) pair with GPT-4o-mini using the prompt in Section D.6. The judge flags three conflict types: (CONTRADICTION), (ALTERNATIVE), and (ENTITY_CONFUSION). Fillers flagged for any type are removed from the pool. 4. At episode assembly time, keyword-based blocklist derived from the current episodes gold entities acts as final safety net. 14 Assistant: Hi! How can help you today? User: My hobby is pottery. Ive been getting into it more lately. Do you know any good techniques for beginners? Assistant: One effective technique for beginners is hand-building, which includes pinch pots, coil building, and slab construction. . . User: drive Zyvanta Sedan. Its been reliable car for me. Do you have any tips on keeping car in good condition? Assistant: To keep your car in good condition, regular maintenance is key: oil changes, fluids, tire pressure, and the manufacturers maintenance schedule. . . User: My health condition is lactose intolerance. It can be bit challenging at times, especially when it comes to choosing what to eat. Have you dealt with lactose intolerance before? Any tips on managing it? Assistant: One effective approach is to explore lactose-free dairy alternatives such as almond, soy, or oat milk, and to check ingredient labels since lactose can be hidden in processed foods. . . User: My sleep usually varies by shift. It can be tough to maintain consistent sleep schedule. Do you have any strategies for managing sleep when working shifts? Assistant: Create relaxing pre-sleep routine, use blackout curtains or earplugs to block out light and noise, and maintain consistent sleep duration when you can. . . User: Ive been watching show called The Quorath Codex. Its been pretty engaging so far. Have you seen it, or do you have any recommendations for similar shows? Assistant: If you enjoy The Quorath Codex, you might also like The Expanse, Shadow and Bone, or The Witcher. . . Figure 6: Full generated session for Episode 1, Session 1 (Fact Introduction) of the Personal Life domain. Each gold fact is introduced by the User LLM in natural conversational turn, followed by topic-appropriate Assistant LLM response (abbreviated). Filter statistics. Table 10 summarizes pool sizes before and after LLM-based conflict filtering. Personal Life is dominated by CONTRADICTION flags while Software Project is dominated by ALTERNATIVE flags, reflecting the different conflict profiles of everyday versus technical content. License attribution. LongMemEval [16] is released under MIT, and the ShareGPT 52K subset [14] is released under CC0 1.0. We redistribute the filtered subsets used in our pipeline as meme-benchmark/MEME-fillers (https://huggingface.co/datasets/meme-benchmark/ MEME-fillers): the structural filtering and curation are released under MIT, while the filler conversation contents pass through under their original sources licenses. The MEME dataset of evaluation episodes is released under CC BY 4.0. Flag counts are per (gold fact, filler) pair; single filler may trigger multiple flags. Rejection examples. Figure 7 shows one rejection example per conflict type, with the conflicting phrases in bold in both the gold fact and the filler excerpt. Assembly and interleaving. Given total filler budget (32K tokens by default) and gaps between evidence sessions, fillers are placed into each gap in shuffled order until each gaps share of roughly B/N tokens is filled. representative 32K-token Personal Life episode consists of 20 filler and 5 evidence sessions arranged as [F 3, E1, 3, E2, 3, E3, 3, E4, 5, E5, 3], where denotes consecutive fillers and E1E5 denote evidence sessions (four Fact Introduction variants followed by Change+Delete Event). Were planning to redo the yard at my family home, which is now fully owned by me and my siblings after finally paying off the mortgage. . . sections.title: C Memory System Configuration
sections.content: For each off-the-shelf system (BM25, text-embedding-3-small, Mem0, Graphiti, Karpathy Wiki), we run with the librarys default configuration. Each systems defaults (top-k, chunk size, retrieval mix, internal-LLM logic) encode design choices that are part of the system itself; unifying them across systems would mask exactly the architectural differences MEME aims to evaluate. Parameters explicitly varied for an ablation are noted where each experiment is described. MD-flat is our own minimal-baseline implementation. Table 11: Per-system overview. Encoding, Maintenance, and Retrieval characterize each systems internal handling at the corresponding pipeline stage. All systems with an internal LLM use gpt-4.1-mini; raw retrieval (BM25, text-embedding-3-small) has none. The external answering LLM is the same gpt-4.1-mini call across all systems (Karpathy Wiki fuses retrieval and answer into single agentic loop). Text highlighting in the table body groups shared stage-level mechanisms: light green for raw-retrieval cells (no internal LLM), light blue for LLM-processed memory, and light orange for file-based tool-use. Per-system descriptions, including the storage substrate, follow below. System Maintenance Encoding Retrieval Raw retrieval BM25 text-embedding-3-small LLM-processed memory Mem0 Graphiti File-based agents MD-flat Karpathy Wiki no LLM no LLM append-only append-only top-5 (lexical) top-5 (cosine) LLM extracts LLM extracts LLM resolves LLM edge invalid. top-20 (vector) top-10 (graph + BM25 + cosine) LLM tool-use LLM tool-use LLM tool-use LLM compile LLM tool-use (read/write/append) LLM navigates index.md BM25. Sparse retrieval using the bm25s library [9] (v0.3.2) with Lucene IDF. Each session is split into 4,096-token chunks (cl100k_base tokenizer, respecting turn boundaries) with [Session: <timestamp>] header prepended. At query time, the top-k=5 chunks are concatenated as retrieval context. No internal LLM is used; the in-memory index is rebuilt incrementally per session and reset between episodes. text-embedding-3-small. Dense retrieval using OpenAI text-embedding-3-small (1,536-dim, L2normalized cosine similarity). Chunking and retrieval follow the same protocol as BM25 (sessionlevel, 4,096-token chunks, top-k=5). No internal LLM is used for ingestion; embedding API cost is reported in Table 5. 16 Mem0. Automatic fact extraction and semantic search over Qdrant vector database. Each session transcript is passed to Memory.add(), which uses an internal LLM to extract discrete facts as natural-language sentences. Mem0s internal LLM logic performs conflict resolution: when new fact contradicts an existing memory, it decides whether to ADD, UPDATE, or DELETE. Retrieval via Memory.search() returns the top-20 most relevant facts (library default for the Mem0 version pinned in our experiment environment; we leave top_k unspecified). Each instance uses unique collection name to prevent cross-episode contamination. Internal LLM: gpt-4.1-mini. Embedding: OpenAI text-embedding-3-small (1,536-dim, Mem0 default). Graphiti. temporal knowledge graph system storing entities and relationships in Neo4j. Each session triggers: (1) entity extraction, (2) edge extraction (triples with fact descriptions), (3) entity deduplication, (4) edge deduplication. Each edge includes temporal metadata (valid_at, invalid_at). Retrieval combines semantic search, BM25 keyword search, and graph traversal (top-10 results). Each episode uses an isolated group_id. key design constraint: Graphitis extraction prompt explicitly instructs closely paraphrase the original source sentence(s). Do not verbatim quote the original text, which directly explains its near-zero Exact Recall accuracy. Internal LLM: gpt-4.1-mini (injected via OpenAIClient). MD-flat. The simplest architecture: an LLM with access to virtual file system containing single markdown file (memory.md). The agent receives each conversation session as input and decides what to store, update, or delete through tool calls (read_file, write_file, append_file, list_files). The ingestion prompt deliberately avoids task-specific hints, making no mention of dependencies, conditional rules, or deletion handling. The tool-calling loop runs for maximum of 5 rounds per operation; in practice, most complete in 23 rounds. Internal LLM: gpt-4.1-mini. Karpathy Wiki. An implementation of Karpathys LLM knowledge base concept [7] using the claude-memory-compiler project3 without modification to its prompts or logic. Three-stage pipeline: Flush (ingest): Each session is passed to flush.py, which uses single LLM call (no tool use) to extract important knowledge and append it to daily log file. Compile (consolidation): After ingesting all sessions, compile.py processes the daily logs into structured knowledge articles organized into three categories: concepts, connections, and qa. An index.md file serves as catalog. The compile operation runs with max_turns=30 and has access to file tools (Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep). Query (retrieval + answer): At question time, query.py reads index.md to identify relevant articles, reads them, and generates an answer in single agentic loop (max_turns=15). Karpathy Wiki uses its native query pipeline for answer generation rather than the unified gpt-4.1-mini answering LM; the cost is reflected in the Retrieve column of Table 5. Each episode runs in an isolated workspace. Internal LLM: gpt-4.1-mini.

sections.title: D Prompts
sections.content: D.1 Verbalization Prompts The conversation generation pipeline converts structured episode skeletons into natural multi-turn conversations using three-stage process. First, gold fact seeds (written in first-person) are batchconverted to third-person statements by an LLM. This intermediate representation is needed because the subsequent self-chat User LLM must receive objective fact descriptions rather than first-person utterances, which it then re-verbalizes naturally. Second, User LLM and an Assistant LLM alternate in self-chat to produce the conversation. Domain-specific third-person prompts are used for each domain: 3https://github.com/coleam00/claude-memory-compiler 17 You are rewriting first-person fact statements into third-person statements about the user. You will receive list of first-person fact seeds. Rewrite each into natural, grammatically correct thirdperson sentence about the user. STRICT RULES: Convert first-person (I, my, we, our) to third-person (the user, the users, they, their) Fix verb conjugation: work The user works, have The user has Fix grammar issues: articles (a/an/the), prepositions, verb forms, capitalization Fix semantic mismatches: e.g., watching show called podcast listening to podcast called... Keep ALL proper nouns, numbers, codes, and entity values EXACTLY as given (e.g., Velthari Studio, 10-to-7 fixed, RSV-09831-THETA must appear verbatim) Keep dependency/causal relationships intact Each output must be single self-contained sentence Do NOT add information not present in the seed Do NOT merge multiple seeds into one sentence Do NOT change punctuation within entity values Output format: JSON object with key facts containing an array of rewritten sentences. Figure 8: Third-person conversion prompt (Personal Life). You are rewriting first-person project statements into third-person statements about the developer or the team. You will receive list of first-person fact seeds about software project. Rewrite each into natural, grammatically correct third-person sentence. STRICT RULES: Convert we/our to the team/the teams, to the developer Fix verb conjugation: We use The team uses Keep ALL tool names, commands, URLs, connection strings, and codes EXACTLY as given (e.g., Veltrion, karnex build prod, crysthene://prod-db:5432/aurora must appear verbatim) Keep dependency/causal relationships intact Each output must be single self-contained sentence Do NOT add information not present in the seed Do NOT merge multiple seeds into one sentence Output format: JSON object with key facts containing an array of rewritten sentences. Figure 9: Third-person conversion prompt (Software Project). After third-person conversion, the User LLM receives these facts and re-verbalizes them as firstperson user in natural conversation: 18 will give you list of personal facts. Use them to act as normal user chatting with chat assistant. In the chat, you may ask it to assist you with various tasks or ask it about various information. However, make sure that you convey the facts about you one by one, roughly in order. IMPORTANT: convey at most ONE fact per message. Do not combine multiple facts into single message. After conveying each fact, continue discussing that topic for 23 more exchanges before moving to the next fact. Ask follow-up questions, request more details, or explore related aspects of the topic. Not every message needs to contain new fact. Make sure your message is concise (13 sentences), since real users often do not bother to write long message. You must simulate the tone of neutral user and do not be overly enthusiastic, verbose, formal, or polite. Do NOT say thanks, will do that, or is there anything else you are user, not an assistant. Do NOT introduce personal facts about yourself beyond the list provided. Only convey the facts given below do not invent new hobbies, family details, health conditions, jobs, living situations, or any other personal information. Follow-up questions should be about the TOPIC, not about yourself. VERBALIZATION RULES (follow strictly): 1. Each fact must include its entity keyword (e.g., My health condition is..., My financial goal is...). The entity concept must appear in your message. 2. If fact mentions dependency (depends on, determined by, if changes), you MUST preserve the full dependency language including the conditional part. The reader must understand that if the source changes, the target would change too. Do NOT weaken depends on X; if changes, this would change to just tied to or since we use X. 3. If fact starts with If (conditional), use ONLY will as the modal: If changes, will be Z. NEVER use would/might/probably/consider. NEVER state it as accomplished (I switched to Z). 4. If fact is marked with [CHANGED], state the new value as definitive current fact. Do NOT hedge or speculate. You are HUMAN user, not an AI. Never say anything that implies you are an AI, language model, or that you have training data cutoff. Never repeat previous message verbatim. Each message must be unique. When ALL facts have been conveyed AND you have finished discussing the last topic, generate exactly: [END] Facts to convey: {facts_list} Figure 10: User LLM system prompt for self-chat conversation generation. The Assistant LLM responds naturally without introducing unsupported assumptions: Respond helpfully and naturally to the users message. Write in natural prose paragraphs do NOT use numbered lists, bullet points, or bold headers. Keep your response to 36 sentences. IMPORTANT: You must NOT assume, guess, or mention any of the following about the user unless the user explicitly stated it in THIS conversation: Family/relationships (partner, spouse, parents, siblings) Living situation (city, housing type, roommates) Work details (company type, industry, salary, colleagues) Health/medical info (conditions, medications, diet reasons) Financial details (income, savings amounts, debt) Schedule/routine specifics beyond what user said Hobbies, pets, vehicles, schools beyond what user said You MAY ask clarifying questions about the topic at hand. You MAY give general advice and information. Never say dont have personal experiences, As an AI, or similar disclaimers. Respond naturally as knowledgeable assistant. Figure 11: Assistant LLM system prompt for self-chat conversation generation (Personal Life). 19 Respond helpfully and naturally to the developers message about their project. Write in natural prose do NOT use numbered lists, bullet points, or bold headers. Keep your response to 36 sentences. IMPORTANT: You must NOT assume, guess, or mention any of the following unless the developer explicitly stated it in THIS conversation: Tool names, frameworks, databases, or libraries not mentioned URLs, connection strings, API keys, or endpoints not shared Team members, leads, or contacts not introduced CI/CD configs, Docker images, or deploy commands not stated Branching strategies, sprint details, or meeting schedules not mentioned You MAY ask clarifying questions about the setup. You MAY give general engineering advice. Never say dont have personal experiences, As an AI, or similar disclaimers. Respond naturally as knowledgeable assistant. Figure 12: Assistant LLM system prompt for self-chat conversation generation (Software Project). D.2 Verification Prompts Annotation prompt. After self-chat generation, an LLM annotates which user turn conveyed each fact: You are annotating conversation to identify where personal facts were conveyed. Given list of facts (in third-person) and conversation, determine which user turn conveys each fact. fact is conveyed if the users message communicates the same information, even if worded differently. Rules: Only consider USER turns (not assistant turns) fact is conveyed if the core information matches, even with different wording. E.g., Fact The user works at Zypherix Labs matches user saying just started at Zypherix Labs If fact appears in multiple turns, return the FIRST (earliest) turn If fact was NOT conveyed in any turn, set turn_index to null turn_index is 0-based index into the conversation array (counting both user and assistant turns) Output format: JSON object with key annotations containing an array of objects: [{fact_id: 1, conveyed: true, turn_index: 2}, ...] Figure 13: Annotation verification prompt (Layer 1). Gemini semantic audit prompt. separate LLM (Gemini 2.5 Flash) performs five-point audit: You are data quality auditor for MEME, benchmark that evaluates LLM agent memory systems. For each gold fact, check ALL criteria below. Report ONLY failures. 1. VERBALIZATION ACCURACY Is the gold facts value present in the conversation (exactly or close paraphrase)? 2. IF-THEN CONDITIONAL FORM (only for facts with is_if_then=true) Must contain if conditional structure. Trigger entity (dependency_source) must be explicitly mentioned. Modal must be will (not would/might/probably) flag WEAK_MODAL. Must NOT be stated as accomplished fact flag CONDITIONAL_VIOLATED. 3. ENTITY KEYWORD PRESENCE The question asks about an entity using certain words (e.g., Whats my financial goal?). Those words or natural synonym must appear in the conversation where the fact is introduced. FAIL if entity concept never mentioned (e.g., Im paying off debt without financial goal). 4. CASCADE-U DEPENDENCY (only for facts with has_dependency=true AND is_if_then=false) Is the causal link STRONGLY explicit? The message must contain language like depends on, determined by, or if changes, would change. FAIL if dependency is only implied (since we use X, tied to X) rather than explicitly conditional (depends on X; if changes, this would change). 5. GOLD ANSWER FORMAT Does before-delete gold have Yes prefix? (flag PREFIX_ISSUE) Output JSON array of issues: {entity: ..., fact_index: N, check: VERBALIZATION IF_THEN KEYWORD CASCADE_U_DEP GOLD_FORMAT, severity: HIGH MEDIUM LOW, issue: brief description} Severity: HIGH = makes task unsolvable. MEDIUM = may cause false pass/fail. LOW = minor. If ALL pass: {status: ALL_PASS, facts_checked: N} Figure 14: Gemini semantic audit prompt (Layer 2). D.3 Ingestion and Retrieval Prompts (MD-flat) MD-flat is the only memory system whose ingestion and retrieval prompts are designed by the benchmark authors; the other systems use their own built-in logic without modification. The ingestion prompt processes each session and updates memory.md; the retrieval prompt reads back from memory.md at query time. You are personal assistant with persistent memory file (memory.md). After each conversation, save any information the user shared that may be useful in future sessions. Keep it compact one fact per line with timestamp [YYYY/MM/DD]. If information has changed, update it. If something was removed or cancelled, remove the old entry. Do NOT save conversation summaries, assistant responses, or temporary task state. You will now receive conversation session. Read it and update your memory accordingly. Figure 15: MD-flat ingestion prompt. The agent operates through tool-calling loop (max 5 rounds) with read_file, write_file, append_file, and list_files. You have access to read_memory(). Read your memory to find relevant facts. Extract ONLY the facts relevant to answering the question below. Read your memory file first, then return relevant facts as-is (do not rephrase or summarize). If nothing is relevant, say (no relevant facts). Question: {question} Figure 16: MD-flat retrieval prompt. At query time the agent runs tool-calling loop with only read_memory available, extracts the relevant facts from memory.md verbatim, and returns them (or (no relevant facts)) as the retrieval context for the answering LLM. D.4 Answer Prompt All memory systems share single unified answer prompt. After each systems retrieve() method returns context string, the following prompt is used with Claude Sonnet 4: 21 Answer the users question based ONLY on the context provided below. If the information is not in the context, say you dont have that information. Answer with ONLY the value. Do not explain or add context. Context: {context} Question: {question} Figure 17: Unified answer prompt shared across all memory systems. D.

sections.title: Judge Prompts
sections.content: The evaluation framework uses task-specific judge prompts with GPT-4o (temperature 0). Each task type has its own prompt: You are judge evaluating whether an AI agents answer is semantically correct. QUESTION: {question} GOLD: {gold} AGENT: {agent_answer} Does the agents answer contain the correct information matching the gold answer? Rules: Focus on semantic equivalence, not exact wording If the gold value is present in the agents answer, it is correct regardless of any additional information, future possibilities, or extra details the agent mentions Dentist every 6 months; dermatologist monthly (if you change residence) gold is dentist (every 6 months) YES (core answer correct, extra info is irrelevant) 40 minutes = 40 min YES dentist appointment every 6 months = dentist (every 6 months) YES If agent says dont know and gold is specific value NO Output ONLY JSON: {correct: true/false, reason: one sentence} Figure 18: Judge prompt: Before-phase (common). You are judge evaluating whether an AI agent correctly recalls sequence of historical values for an entity that changed multiple times. QUESTION: {question} GOLD (ordered history values, comma-separated): {gold} AGENT: {agent_answer} The users information changed multiple times. The question asks for the FULL HISTORY in chronological order (earliest to latest). Rules: Agents answer must contain ALL gold values in the EXACT order specified Order matters: the values must appear earliest-to-latest as in the gold Extra surrounding text is fine, but the gold sequence must be preserved Missing any value NO Wrong order NO Only some values NO dont know NO Output ONLY JSON: {correct: true/false, reason: one sentence} Figure 19: Judge prompt: Tracking. You are judge evaluating whether an AI agents answer contains specific values. QUESTION: {question} TARGET VALUES: {gold_values} AGENT: {agent_answer} For EACH target value, check whether it appears (exact or paraphrased) in the agents answer. Rules: The value appears in the answer (exact or paraphrased) true The value is absent false dont know or dont have that information all false Output ONLY JSON: {results: [{value: ..., present: true/false}], reason: one sentence} Figure 20: Judge prompt: Aggregation. You are judge evaluating whether an AI agent correctly recalls an EXACT value verbatim. QUESTION: {question} GOLD: {gold} AGENT: {agent_answer} Does the agents answer contain the exact value? Rules: Check if the gold value appears VERBATIM as substring in the agents answer If the gold value is fully contained in the answer, even with extra words before/after it YES Example: gold=OOM killed by container runtime, agent=OOM killed by container runtime errors YES (gold is fully preserved) Minor formatting differences are acceptable (e.g., extra spaces, capitalization) Missing or substituted words WITHIN the gold value NO dont know or dont have that information NO completely different value NO Output ONLY JSON: {correct: true/false, reason: one sentence} Figure 21: Judge prompt: Exact Recall. Note: implemented as deterministic substring match at runtime; this prompt serves as fallback documentation. You are judge evaluating whether an AI agent correctly handles DELETED piece of information. QUESTION: {question} GOLD: {gold} AGENT: {agent_answer} The information was explicitly deleted/removed. The agent should not return it. Rules: Agent says dont have that information / no longer available / Unknown / None / similar WITHOUT revealing the deleted value YES Agent indicates the item no longer exists or applies WITHOUT revealing the deleted value (e.g., No food allergies when the deleted value was specific allergy) YES Agent reveals the deleted value in any way (even while saying it was deleted/removed) NO Agent returns the deleted value as if it still exists NO Output ONLY JSON: {correct: true/false, reason: one sentence} Figure 22: Judge prompt: Deletion. 23 You are judge evaluating whether an AI agent correctly reports CASCADE-DEPENDENT value after an upstream change. QUESTION: {question} GOLD (new cascaded value): {gold} AGENT: {agent_answer} The upstream entity changed, triggering cascade. The agent should now report ONLY the new value. Rules: Agent states the new value as the sole, definitive answer YES Agent lists BOTH old and new values together NO Agent states only the old value NO Agent lists multiple options or hedges (might, considering) NO Agent uses future tense (will change to X, will be X) instead of confirming as current NO dont know NO Output ONLY JSON: {correct: true/false, reason: one sentence} Figure 23: Judge prompt: Cascade. You are judge evaluating whether an AI agent appropriately handles UNCERTAINTY after an upstream entity changed. QUESTION: {question} GOLD: {gold} AGENT: {agent_answer} The upstream entity changed, making the previous answer potentially invalid. The agent should express uncertainty. Rules: Agent says dont know / not sure / Unknown / None / expresses uncertainty YES Agent acknowledges the upstream change and questions validity YES Agent confidently states the OLD value as definitive current fact NO Agent provides the old value with no hedging or uncertainty NO Output ONLY JSON: {correct: true/false, reason: one sentence} Figure 24: Judge prompt: Absence. For Aggregation and Tracking tasks, partial credit is computed in addition to binary pass/fail. Aggregation records the number of target values present out of the total. Tracking records the number of history values in correct chronological order using position-based algorithm. D.6 Filler Filtering Prompt You are checking if filler conversation conflicts with known gold fact. Gold fact: {gold_fact} Filler conversation: {filler} Check these 3 conflict types: A) CONTRADICTION: Does the filler directly contradict the gold fact? B) ALTERNATIVE: Does the filler introduce plausible alternative answer that could confuse memory system? C) ENTITY_CONFUSION: Does the filler mention the same entity/topic in confusing way? Answer in JSON format: {A: true/false, B: true/false, C: true/false, reason: brief explanation or no conflict } Figure 25: Filler conflict judgment prompt (GPT-4o-mini).

sections.title: E Prompt Optimization Details
sections.content: This section provides details on the prompt optimization experiment described in Section 4.4. 24 E.1 Setup and Configuration We used DSPy SIMBA [8], an append-only prompt optimizer that iterates two strategies, rule appending and few-shot demo appending, without modifying or deleting baseline instructions. SIMBA was applied to MD-flat, Mem0, Graphiti, and Karpathy Wiki. For each system, only the ingest and retrieve prompts were optimized; the answer prompt was frozen because its backing model (Claude Sonnet 4) does not support the OpenAI seed parameter, making noise non-suppressible for that component. Multi-seed (N =5) statistics for MD-flat are reported in Section G.2; Mem0, Graphiti, and Karpathy results are single-seed (seed=7). Table 12: Model and optimizer configuration for the prompt optimization experiment. Role Model Temp. Task LM (ingest, retrieve) Answer LM Prompt model (SIMBA meta) Judge gpt-4.1-mini Claude Sonnet 4 gpt-4.1-mini GPT-4o 0 0 0.9 0 SIMBA was configured with max_steps=2, bsize=4, num_candidates=4, and num_threads=4. The data split used seed=7 stratified sampling: 10 training episodes (5 PL + 5 SW) and 10 held-out test episodes (5 PL + 5 SW), disjoint at the episode level. E.2 Optimized Prompt Content SIMBA returned three candidate programs per system; for each, the winning candidate appended advice blocks to the systems prompts (baseline text retained verbatim before these blocks). The appended text per system is shown below. (ingest, appended) If the input involves information about configuration dependencies (such as backup schedule, ORM used, or model syntax), then explicitly encode conditional knowledge linking these dependencies, e.g., if ORM changes from Vorathis ODM to Pyravar Mapper, the model syntax should update accordingly. If the input indicates deletion of an entity (like the onboarding guide), then mark the entity as deleted and respond explicitly that the information is no longer available. When partial or uncertain information is present, avoid generic dont know answers, and instead provide clarifications or acknowledge the absence explicitly. Leverage exact matching and dependency chains to improve recall of critical facts such as exact error logs. If the conversation input includes conditional or dependent information (e.g., living arrangements depend on relationship status), then you should explicitly encode the dependency chains and update or remove old facts accordingly. When change event occurs (like marriage or dietary restriction removal), ensure to mark prior conflicting facts as outdated or deleted and represent the new facts with clear dependency references so that downstream retrieval can reason over them. (retrieve, appended) When receiving queries, especially those involving multiple updates, cascading dependencies, or multi-hop reasoning, focus on retrieving all relevant facts including updated and deleted information to fully answer the question. For multi-hop queries, chain together related facts to assemble comprehensive response. When facts have dependencies or conditionals (e.g., backup schedule depends on the current database), retrieve the full dependency chain. For deletion queries, ensure retrieval reflects removal status so final answers indicate lack of availability rather than ignoring or failing to answer. If query involves entities with known dependencies or recent changes (such as changed relationship status affecting living arrangements and family events), then you should retrieve not only direct facts but also the full dependency chain surrounding these facts. Prioritize the most recent or updated information and infer uncertainty when dependencies conflict or are unresolved. This strategy helps generate consistent, accurate answers, especially for cascading or multi-hop questions. Figure 26: MD-flat: SIMBA-appended advice blocks for the ingest and retrieve prompts. MD-flat. The optimized retrieve prompt contains explicit instructions to infer uncertainty when dependencies conflict or are unresolved, directly targeting the Absence task. Despite this, Absence accuracy drops to 0.00, suggesting that LLMs do not reliably internalize meta-cognitive instructions about expressing uncertainty. 25 Mem0. SIMBA returned the unchanged Mem0 library default (ADDITIVE_EXTRACTION_PROMPT, 33K chars with default examples) as the winning candidate. The two alternative candidates that did append rule blocks scored lower on training (0.509 and 0.501 vs. baseline 0.545), so SIMBA selected the default. Test accuracy moved 0.545 0.534 (1.1pp), within the noise floor reported next. Graphiti. SIMBA appended advice paragraphs to each of (extract_message, edge, dedupe_nodes). The appended texts are shown below. the three Graphiti prompts (extract_message, appended) If the module receives conversation messages containing technical or configuration details such as database connection strings, ORM model syntaxes, backup schedules, or package manager names, then it should prioritize explicit and accurate extraction of these distinct factual entities. It should distinguish between different software components and configurations even if phrased similarly. Being explicit in naming and careful in identifying such entities will improve downstream knowledge integration. If the module receives conversation messages containing explicit conditional or dependent living arrangements, then it should explicitly extract and represent all relevant entities, including conditional entities like sibling tied to relationship status changes. The module should robustly disambiguate references and update entity extraction as new facts (e.g., changes in relationship status) are introduced to avoid missing or outdated entities. (edge, appended) If the module receives messages and an entity list containing technical terms like connection strings, model syntax, or backup schedules, then it should extract precise fact triples that link these entities with their values and contextual metadata, including temporality and versioning if applicable. It should avoid hallucination and ensure relations reflect the actual statements in the conversation, supporting correct temporal or conditional dependencies (e.g., changes of configurations over time). If the module receives entity lists and conversation messages with conditional or evolving personal information, then it should ensure that fact triples clearly capture these conditional relationships and that responses to queries reflect the current, updated state or acknowledge uncertainty appropriately. The module must avoid hallucinating unsupported entities or facts and must ground edges in the explicitly available and valid entities, maintaining consistent temporal and logical coherence. (dedupe_nodes, appended) If the module receives extracted entities and existing knowledge base entities, then it should resolve duplicates strictly based on identity and completeness without merging distinct entities or omitting valid ones. It should preserve distinct technical entities especially configuration parameters and versions that are critical for correctness and traceability in knowledge representation, thereby preventing loss of information critical to later query answering. If the module receives newly extracted entities and updated knowledge base entities reflecting changed or deleted facts, then it should carefully resolve duplicates only for valid, consistent entities, respecting conditional dependencies and removing outdated facts. The module should strictly avoid merging distinct entities or preserving obsolete information, particularly when explicit deletions or updates are available, ensuring an accurate entity inventory aligned with the latest user context. Figure 27: Graphiti: SIMBA-appended advice blocks for the three optimized prompts. Karpathy Wiki. SIMBA appended one advice paragraph to each of the three Karpathy Wiki prompts (flush, compile, query). The appended texts are shown below. 26 (flush, appended) If the module receives raw conversation data containing key project facts, then it should focus on extracting and summarizing actionable, non-trivial information precisely and completely. It should emphasize capturing explicit facts about configurations, deployments, and changes, and flagging any deletions or updates to ensure downstream modules have accurate logs to compile from. (compile, appended) If the module receives daily logs including configuration details and knowledge updates, then it should rigorously extract all relevant factual knowledge, including explicit mappings of infrastructure dependencies and conditional changes. It should represent these with clarity in the wiki, explicitly noting dependencies and if-then scenarios, and carefully documenting deletions or overrides. Complete YAML frontmatter and linking related concepts in the knowledge base are essential for quality. (query, appended) If the module receives questions or queries about configuration, deployment, or error logs, then it should perform context-aware retrieval prioritizing exact matches and dependency-aware reasoning. It should handle deletions properly, recognize conditional dependencies (e.g., if deploy target changes, then CI config changes), and be capable of precise recall for exact error logs. Summarizing complex dependencies succinctly while citing relevant articles or sources can improve answer accuracy and completeness. Figure 28: Karpathy Wiki: SIMBA-appended advice blocks for the three optimized prompts. Per-task stability statistics for the SIMBA baseline-vs-optimized comparison are reported in Section G.2, computed in the same =5 format as Table 14.

sections.content: We evaluate three representative systems (MD-flat, Mem0, and text-embedding-3-small) under three filler conditions: no filler (evidence sessions only), 32K tokens (default), and 128K tokens (4 default). Each condition uses the same 40 episodes (20 PL + 20 SW). Deletion, Cascade, and Absence scores use the same trivial-pass filtering as the main results table. 27 1 0.5 r A 0. 0.78 0.7 0.53 0.55 0.57 0. MD-flat 0.97 0.85 0.47 0.2 0. No filler 32K 128K

sections.title: Absence
sections.content: Figure 29: Per-task accuracy under three filler conditions (no filler, 32K, 128K) for each system. Deletion, Cascade, and Absence use trivial-pass filtering (same convention as the main results table). General trend (32K 128K). All three systems show overall degradation when filler volume increases from 32K to 128K: MD-flat drops from 0.45 to 0.36, Mem0 from 0.26 to 0.22, and text-embedding-3-small from 0.23 to 0.16. For Mem0 and text-embedding-3-small, the decline is concentrated in retrieval tasks (Aggregation, Exact Recall), where increased filler dilutes the signalto-noise ratio in semantic search. MD-flats decline is broader, affecting Aggregation (0.20) and Tracking (0.20), as the agents monolithic memory file grows proportionally with filler volume. No-filler anomaly for MD-flat. Counter-intuitively, MD-flats overall accuracy is lower without filler (0.40) than with 32K filler (0.45). Inspection reveals that the retrieve step (gpt-4.1-mini) behaves differently when the memory file is short: it strips timestamps and entity labels, returning bare bullet lists (e.g., - User drives Zyvanta Sedan instead of [2023/03/01] User drives Zyvanta Sedan). The answering LLM (Claude Sonnet 4), following its strict context-only instruction, then refuses to infer chronological order or entity provenance, causing failures on Tracking and Exact Recall. With 32K filler, the memory file is longer and more diverse, prompting the retrieve step to preserve metadata. This artifact is specific to the retrieve prompts sensitivity to memory file length rather than property of the benchmark itself. 28 Cascade and Absence remain at floor. Across all three noise conditions and all three systems, Cascade and Absence scores remain near zero (0.000.19), reinforcing the main finding that under the default configuration the dependency-reasoning gap is not driven by retrieval noise. Trivial-pass rates rise with noise (justifies the filter). The trivial-pass filter discards Absence/Deletion/Cascade passes that came from system that never knew the original value (i.e., before-statecheck failed). Table 13 shows the trivial-pass count per (system, condition, task). For raw retrieval (text-embedding-3-small), the Absence trivial-pass rate rises sharply with noise (0.08 0.23 0.51): under heavy filler the top-k retrieved chunks are dominated by irrelevant content, the answering LLM defaults to dont know, and dont know happens to match the Absence gold of uncertain. Without the trivial-pass filter, this artifact would inflate text-embedding-3-smalls Absence score from 0.11 to 0.35 across the same conditions; with the filter, the score stays at roughly 0.110.15, matching the main finding that the dependency gap is not closed by retrieval failure. Table 13: Trivial-pass counts (and rates) per condition for the three noise-evaluated systems on the 40-episode subset. trivial pass is question where the system answered correctly post-change but failed the pre-change state-check, indicating it never knew the original value. The trivial-pass filter excludes these from the numerator of Cascade, Absence, and Deletion accuracy. We rerun two configurations under =5 identical trials each on 10-episode subset (5 PL + 5 SW) to (i) confirm the main-table single-run results are not sampling artifacts and (ii) calibrate the noise floor for the SIMBA prompt-optimization comparison in Section E. Raw retrieval (BM25, text-embedding-3-small) is deterministic and excluded throughout. Trivial-pass filtering applies to Cascade, Absence, and Deletion as in the main results. G.1 Main-table baseline We rerun ingestion, retrieval, and answering on the four systems with non-deterministic LLM components (Mem0, Graphiti, MD-flat, Karpathy Wiki) under the main-table configuration (gpt-4.1mini internal LLM, Claude Sonnet 4 answering LLM); =5 trials, including the original main run on the same 10 episodes plus four additional sampling trials. Karpathy Wiki shows the largest task-level variance (Tracking SD 0.15), driven by its agentic query loop with multiple internal LLM calls per question; the other systems retrieve and answer in fixed number of LLM calls and show smaller variance. G.2 SIMBA prompt-optimization (MD-flat) For the prompt-optimization experiment described in Section E, we re-evaluate the baseline and SIMBA-optimized configurations on the same 10-episode SIMBA test set under =5 trials each. In contrast, Cascade and Absence differences (0.05 and 0.03) sit within the SD of either configuration. Answering LLM Swap: Per-System Breakdown Table 16 reports the full per-task accuracy for the answering-LLM swap summarized in Table 3. We use the datasets gold-fact metadata to identify, for each task, the specific evidence sessions carrying the rule and the change event. The rule session contains gold-fact entry with entity = target_entity and (Cascade) is_if_then = True or (Absence) has_dependency = True. The change session is the canonical evidence_change+delete_event session, with gold-fact entry where entity = cascade_source and type = root_change. For each rule and change fact, the original_seed string appears verbatim in its sessions conversation in 100% of cases (213 rule, 100 change, 341 dependency facts in the 100-episode dataset), so checking for original_seed as substring of the retrieval context is exact. Identical breakdowns are obtained by matching the sessions [Session: <timestamp>] header instead, confirming that no rule/change content is split off from its session header by chunking. Results. Table 17 partitions knew_but_failed cases by which stage missed the propagated answer: change-event missing from retrieval, rule missing from retrieval, or both retrieved but the answering LLM fails to propagate. The rule is retrieved in 100% of failures across all k, so retrieval-side losses are change-event misses; the live trade-off at higher is between change-event miss and answering failure. Cascade: at k=20 and k=40, 55% of failures are change-event misses and 45% are answering failures (the rule and the change session are both retrieved yet the answering LLM still does not produce the propagated value).
